meta.title: Kaushik Baidya | Embedded Software Engineer
meta.description: Embedded software engineer specialising in RTOS, Linux device drivers, automotive ECU software, and real-time systems.

bio: Building reliable software at the intersection of hardware and operating systems. 5 years specialising in RTOS, Embedded Linux, automotive ECU development, and real-time control systems.

journey.title: Engineering Evolution
journey.desc: A structured progression through embedded systems - from bare metal C to automotive-grade software.

skills.title: Competency Matrix
skills.desc: Technical depth across languages, platforms, protocols, and toolchains - each rated by production experience.

stats.title: Engineering Metrics
stats.desc: Production experience at a glance - projects shipped, technologies mastered, and open-source contributions.

layers.label: Application Software
layers.detail: User-defined application code. Calls HAL and RTOS APIs. Defines tasks, interrupt handlers, and system behaviour. Should have no direct register access.

layers.label: RTOS
layers.detail: FreeRTOS provides preemptive multitasking, semaphores, queues, mutexes, and software timers. Tick interrupt drives the scheduler. Configurable priority levels and heap schemes.

layers.label: HAL / Driver Layer
layers.sub: Peripheral abstraction - UART, SPI, I2C, CAN
layers.detail: Hardware Abstraction Layer isolates the application from MCU-specific register addresses. Enables portability across STM32 families and other ARM Cortex-M devices.

layers.label: CMSIS
layers.sub: Core access, NVIC, SysTick, DSP intrinsics
layers.detail: Cortex Microcontroller Software Interface Standard - ARM-defined API for core peripherals (NVIC, SysTick, MPU) and compiler-independent register access.

layers.label: Silicon / Hardware
layers.detail: Physical hardware: ARM Cortex-M core, flash, SRAM, peripheral IPs (USART, SPI, I2C, ADC, TIM, DMA), and external components connected via communication buses.

layers.label: User Space Applications
layers.detail: systemd is PID 1. It reads unit files and brings up services in parallel. Application daemons run as regular processes with no kernel privilege.

layers.label: C Library (glibc / musl)
layers.sub: POSIX API bridge between userspace and kernel
layers.detail: Provides standard C library functions and wraps Linux syscalls. musl-libc is preferred in embedded Yocto images for its small size and strict POSIX compliance.

layers.label: Linux Kernel
layers.detail: Loaded by U-Boot at the physical address specified in the FIT image. The kernel decompresses, initialises architecture code, parses the device tree, and brings up drivers via the probe/match mechanism.

layers.label: U-Boot
layers.sub: Loads kernel + DTB, handles OTA A/B swap
layers.detail: U-Boot initialises DRAM, establishes a boot environment, and loads the kernel FIT image from eMMC/SD/TFTP. On platforms with secure boot, U-Boot verifies FIT signature before handing off.

layers.label: U-Boot SPL / MLO
layers.detail: Secondary Program Loader runs entirely from on-chip SRAM (before DRAM is initialised). Its sole job: configure PLLs and DRAM controller, load U-Boot into DRAM, jump.

layers.label: ROM Boot Code
layers.sub: On-chip ROM - finds and verifies SPL
layers.detail: Burned-in ROM on the SoC. Enumerates boot sources (eMMC, SD, UART, USB), reads the MLO/SPL image, optionally verifies its signature against an OTP-stored hash, and jumps.

layers.label: Application Layer (ASW)
layers.detail: Application Software Components (SWCs) communicate only through the VFB (Virtual Functional Bus). Runnables are triggered by timing or data events, isolated from hardware.

layers.label: RTE (Runtime Environment)
layers.sub: Generated glue - connects SWCs to BSW
layers.detail: Auto-generated by AUTOSAR toolchain. Routes inter-SWC communication and connects SWCs to BSW services (NvM, COM, Dcm). Acts as the "virtual bus" implementation.

layers.label: Services Layer
layers.detail: High-level BSW services independent of the ECU hardware. NvM manages non-volatile data, WdgM monitors watchdogs, Dcm handles UDS diagnostic requests.

layers.label: ECU Abstraction Layer
layers.detail: Abstracts ECU-specific hardware. IoHwAb provides signal-level ADC/DIO/PWM access. Communication stacks (CanIf, LinIf) provide bus-type-independent message routing.

layers.label: MCAL (Microcontroller Abstraction)
layers.sub: Port, Dio, Adc, Pwm, Spi, Can, Lin drivers
layers.detail: Vendor-specific but AUTOSAR-compliant drivers that directly access the MCU registers. MCAL is the only layer with register-level code - all layers above it are hardware-independent.

layers.label: Microcontroller Hardware
layers.detail: Physical ECU hardware. MCAL configuration (ARXML) defines pin mapping, clock frequencies, interrupt priorities, and peripheral mode - no hardcoding above this layer.

layers.label: Application / Diagnostic Layer
layers.detail: Application protocol riding over CAN frames. UDS (ISO 14229) provides diagnostic services. J1939 adds addressing and fragmentation for heavy-duty vehicles. CANopen for industrial control.

layers.label: ISO-TP (ISO 15765-2)
layers.detail: Handles segmentation and reassembly of data payloads larger than one CAN frame. Used by UDS for reading/writing large data objects (e.g., ECU flash download, VIN).

layers.label: CAN Data Link Layer
layers.detail: CAN 2.0A/B / CAN FD framing: SOF, arbitration ID, control, data, CRC, ACK, EOF. CSMA/CD arbitration, bit-stuffing, 3-layer error detection (bit, stuff, CRC).

layers.label: CAN Physical Layer
layers.detail: Two-wire differential signalling (CAN_H / CAN_L). Transceivers (TJA1042, MCP2562) convert MCU CAN TX/RX logic levels to the differential bus. 120 ohm termination at each bus end.
